text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how Redwood’s tier-aware routing and overload protection work for **Dedicated** deployments, and how to operate/tune these controls to preserve **Dedicated throughput guarantees** under contention.

It is written for:
- Eng Platform (routing/control plane)
- SRE (on-call responders)
- Infra/Fleet (pool health/capacity)

## Scope
In scope:
- Tier-to-pool routing behavior (Standard / Plus / Premium)
- Admission control (queue caps, token budget gating)
- Overload behaviors (shedding, brownout, fallback posture)
- Operational procedures (config changes, feature flags, rollback)

Out of scope:
- Customer-facing comms and credits policy (see escalation playbook)
- Hardware provisioning steps (see Dedicated capacity provisioning runbook)
- SLO semantics and reporting (see Dedicated throughput SLO definition)

## Definitions

text:
## Summary
This guide describes how to integrate any Redwood service (API Gateway, Control Plane, Console backend, SDK edge services, billing/metering hooks) with the **Checklist Progress Service (CPS)** that powers the in-Console onboarding and “first production launch” checklist.

It covers:
- Where to emit events (server-side vs client-side)
- CPS event ingestion APIs and required fields
- Service-to-service auth requirements
- Idempotency and out-of-order delivery handling
- Backfill patterns (existing tenants, legacy signals)
- Failure modes and operational expectations

This page is the implementation reference; for canonical naming/fields, use **Onboarding Event Schema v1** and the ADR linked above.

---

## Definitions
**Tenant**: Redwood customer account / org (primary key: `tenant_id`).

**Checklist step**: A user-visible milestone (e.g., “Create an API key”, “Make first suc...

title: Saturation signals standardization (Hosted + Dedicated pools)
preview:
## Summary
This page defines Redwood’s standardized *saturation metrics* across Hosted and Dedicated GPU pools. The goal is to make saturation signals:
- Comparable across deployment modes (Hosted shared pools vs Dedicated reserved pools)
- Bounded in cardinality and query cost
- Actionable (clear thresholds + “what...
text:
## Summary
This page defines Redwood’s standardized *saturation metrics* across Hosted and Dedicated GPU pools. The goal is to make saturation signals:
- Comparable across deployment modes (Hosted shared pools vs Dedicated reserved pools)
- Bounded in cardinality and query cost
- Actionable (clear thresholds + “what to do next”)
- Consumable by Incident Bot v2 context cards and Console dashboards

This standard is required for any service/pool that serves online inference traffic (chat/generate/embeddings/rerank).

## Why we’re standardizing
Historically, teams used a mix of node-exporter GPU metrics, runtime-specific counters, and ad hoc dashboard panels. During incidents, responders lost time reconciling:
- Which metric to trust (GPU util vs queue vs KV cache pressure)
- Which labels/filters to use (cluster vs pool vs region)
- Which thresholds represent “true saturation” vs normal ...

## Overview
This page describes the internal procedure to enable the **Hosted canary rollout workflow** for a specific tenant. This includes:
- Feature-flag enablement (Console + API + data plane enforcement)
- Required RBAC permissions/roles
- Sanity checks to run before handing off to the customer (beta and GA)
- How to disable / back out safely

**Scope:** Hosted routes only. Dedicated/Private are out of scope for enablement and should not be promised as “enabled” by these steps.

**Primary owners:** Eng Platform (control plane + orchestrator), Console (UI), Smart Routing (data plane), SRE (SLO + auto-stop), Security (audit logs + RBAC).

---

## Definitions
- **Tenant:** Redwood customer account (organization) in the control plane.

preview:

Route experiments are evaluated at request-routing time. Each eligible request is ...
text:
## Purpose
This page describes the operator workflow to create, start, ramp, and stop **route-level experiments** (A/B/n) in Redwood’s routing layer. It includes recommended guardrails, staged ramp practices, and safe rollback steps.

Route experiments are evaluated at request-routing time. Each eligible request is deterministically assigned to a variant based on a stickiness key, and routing then selects the corresponding model/serving policy for that variant.

---

## Audience
- Platform / on-call engineers operating Redwood routing and control plane.
- Solutions engineers supporting customer rollouts.
- Console operators managing experiments on behalf of internal teams.

---

## Key concepts (operators)
### Experiment
A named configuration attached to one or more **routes** with:
- **Variants** (A/B/n): each variant maps to a model version and/or policy bundle.
- **Allocation**: tr...

title: Cache hit-rate instrumentation sprint retro (prefix/KV cache telemetry)
preview:
## Summary
This retrospective covers the focused sprint to ship prefix/KV cache telemetry that is (a) reliable, (b) low overhead, (c) privacy-safe, and (d) actionable for Runtime/SRE/Platform and the Recommendations MVP.

Overall outcome: we shipped the core runtime instrumentation, ingestion-side cardinality contro...
text:
## Summary
This retrospective covers the focused sprint to ship prefix/KV cache telemetry that is (a) reliable, (b) low overhead, (c) privacy-safe, and (d) actionable for Runtime/SRE/Platform and the Recommendations MVP.

Overall outcome: we shipped the core runtime instrumentation, ingestion-side cardinality controls, initial dashboards/alerts, and a rollout plan with canary gates. We did not fully land the recommendation batch job end-to-end in prod (still MVP-ready but missing backtesting + Console stub integration). We also discovered several correctness edge cases (stream retries, continuous batching attribution) that were fixed during the sprint but highlight ongoing tech debt in request lifecycle accounting.

## Sprint window
- Sprint start: 2025-03-03 (go/no-go approved)
- Sprint end: 2025-03-21
- Retro: 2025-03-24

## Goals (from sprint brief)
1) Establish a reliable, low-ove...

## Summary
The **Experiment Service** (internal name: `experiment-svc`) is the control-plane component that stores and validates route-level experiment configuration and publishes a fast, deterministic representation to the policy/routing layer.

This document covers:

Route-level A/B testing requires customers to define experiments at the **route** boundary (endpoint + traffic policy), assign requests deterministically to a **variant**, and then attribute telemetry by `{route, experiment_id, variant}`.

title: Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service (Control Plane)
preview:
## Overview
The **Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service** (service name: `dedicated-pool-rollouts`) is the control-plane component that manages **staged rollout plans** for **Dedicated GPU capacity pools**. It provides APIs, persistence, and auditability for creating and executing staged rollouts of:
- Model versions (e.g...
text:
## Overview
The **Dedicated Pool Rollouts Service** (service name: `dedicated-pool-rollouts`) is the control-plane component that manages **staged rollout plans** for **Dedicated GPU capacity pools**. It provides APIs, persistence, and auditability for creating and executing staged rollouts of:
- Model versions (e.g., `llama-3.1-70b@2025-03-xx`)
- Serving runtime builds (e.g., runtime image / kernel set)
- Routing policies (e.g., fallback policy changes)
- Pool-level configuration changes that materially affect production traffic

This service is responsible for **deterministic, auditable, reversible** rollout control. It does **not** directly route traffic; instead it publishes rollout state to the policy/routing layer which enforces stage partitions at request time.

Primary users:
- Redwood SRE/Operators running Dedicated pool upgrades
- Console workflows (customer admins and Redwo...

title: RBAC v2 rollout runbook (Hosted, Dedicated, Private)
preview:
h1. RBAC v2 rollout runbook

This runbook describes how to safely roll out RBAC v2 enforcement across Redwood’s API Gateway, Control Plane services, and Console/admin tooling.

RBAC v2 is *deny-by-default* and is rolled out via feature flags and policy bundle versioning. The rollout strategy is:
1) prove correctness...
text:
h1. RBAC v2 rollout runbook

This runbook describes how to safely roll out RBAC v2 enforcement across Redwood’s API Gateway, Control Plane services, and Console/admin tooling.

RBAC v2 is *deny-by-default* and is rolled out via feature flags and policy bundle versioning. The rollout strategy is:
1) prove correctness in *dual-read* (v1 + v2 evaluated, v1 enforced)
2) enable v2 enforcement in a staged way (read-only → write endpoints; internal → pilot → GA)
3) monitor denial rates, error rates, latency, and audit log correctness
4) maintain fast rollback paths that restore RBAC v1 enforcement within minutes

h2. Scope

In-scope systems for this runbook:
- API Gateway (request authz middleware, standardized 403/denied responses)
- Control Plane services (endpoint annotations + interceptors)
- Console (role management UX + “why denied” experience)
- Policy bundle repo + policy distributio...

title: Cache Telemetry Pipeline Incident Runbook (Prefix/KV cache metrics + traces)
preview:
## Purpose
This runbook covers incidents where **cache telemetry** (prefix cache + KV cache) stops flowing, drops to zero, becomes inconsistent, or shows obvious data-quality failures.

Telemetry in scope:
- Metrics: hit/miss counters, miss-reason breakdown, reuse histograms, eviction/pressure gauges, wasted prefill...
text:
## Purpose
This runbook covers incidents where **cache telemetry** (prefix cache + KV cache) stops flowing, drops to zero, becomes inconsistent, or shows obvious data-quality failures.

Telemetry in scope:
- Metrics: hit/miss counters, miss-reason breakdown, reuse histograms, eviction/pressure gauges, wasted prefill.
- Tracing: request spans with cache annotations (low-cardinality events/attributes).

Primary outcomes:
1) Restore telemetry flow and correctness.
2) Distinguish **real cache regressions** vs **telemetry/pipeline failures**.
3) Mitigate quickly with safe knobs (sampling/rate limits/label drops) without impacting runtime latency.

---

## Owners, oncall, and escalation
**Primary owners (business hours):**
- Telemetry pipelines: Logan Wright (EM, Telemetry), Nadia Rahman (schema)
- Ingestion/cardinality controls: Caleb Johnson
- Runtime instrumentation: Omar El-Sayed (lead)...

title: Cache recommendations batch job (MVP) — design
preview:
## Overview
This document describes the MVP batch job that aggregates prefix/KV cache telemetry and emits “increase hit-rate” recommendations for internal operators (SRE/Runtime/Platform) and an initial Console/Optimize surface.

The job is intentionally conservative: it prefers fewer, higher-confidence recommendati...
text:
## Overview
This document describes the MVP batch job that aggregates prefix/KV cache telemetry and emits “increase hit-rate” recommendations for internal operators (SRE/Runtime/Platform) and an initial Console/Optimize surface.

The job is intentionally conservative: it prefers fewer, higher-confidence recommendations over noisy output. It is designed to be privacy-safe (no raw prompts) and operationally boring (idempotent, bounded cardinality, and easy to reason about when data is missing).

## Goals (MVP)
1. Produce per-route (and optionally per-region/model) cache-efficiency summaries from validated telemetry.
2. Convert summaries into actionable recommendations with:
 - a clear “what changed / what’s wrong”
 - a likely root cause (miss reason / pressure signal)
 - a concrete lever (config, routing, canonicalization, TTL/policy)
 - a confidence score and severity
3. Support intern...

- **SRE oncall** triage for regressions (hit-rate drops, eviction pressure, telemetry gaps)
- **Runtime/Platform** debugging (miss reason att...
text:
## Summary
This page defines the **Grafana dashboard set** and **alert rules** for the new prefix/KV cache telemetry emitted by the serving runtime. It is intended to support:
- **SRE oncall** triage for regressions (hit-rate drops, eviction pressure, telemetry gaps)
- **Runtime/Platform** debugging (miss reason attribution, request-shape sensitivity)
- **Product/Optimize** validation (is caching effective enough to recommend changes?)

This spec is deliberately opinionated about:
- **Filters and breakdowns** that match how Redwood operates (model/route/deployment/region/request shape)
- **Cardinality controls** (avoid per-tenant explosions; use allowlisted route/model identifiers)
- **Operational readiness** (panels that distinguish real behavior changes vs missing telemetry)

---

## Audience and primary use-cases

- Runtime (cache + schedul...

text:
## Purpose
This runbook describes how to operate **Dedicated staged rollouts** for **reserved-capacity pools** ("Dedicated pools"). It covers:
- Creating and validating a DedicatedPoolRolloutPlan
- Running and promoting stages
- Interpreting gate failures (including missing/lagging telemetry)
- Manual override procedures (hold / force-promote / abort)
- Emergency rollback steps and what to do during incidents

Staged rollouts are used to safely deploy changes to a Dedicated pool, including:
- Model version upgrades (weights/tokenizer)
- Serving runtime build changes
- Routing policy changes (e.g., fallback behavior)
- Performance/caching/batching configuration changes

## Scope and non-goals
**In scope:** operational procedures and safety controls for rollouts that partition a Dedicated pool into stage segments (traffic or replica partitions) with measurable gates.

**Not a general ex...

title: Rollout Orchestrator Service (Hosted Canary Rollouts)
preview:
## Overview
The **Rollout Orchestrator** is the control-plane service responsible for the lifecycle of **Hosted canary rollouts** (percentage-based traffic splitting on Hosted routes). It owns the rollout state machine, validates progression against policy guardrails, persists rollout state, and emits events used by...
text:
## Overview
The **Rollout Orchestrator** is the control-plane service responsible for the lifecycle of **Hosted canary rollouts** (percentage-based traffic splitting on Hosted routes). It owns the rollout state machine, validates progression against policy guardrails, persists rollout state, and emits events used by:

- **Smart Routing / Data Plane** to enforce the current traffic split and cohort assignment
- **Console backend** to drive the UI workflow (wizard, timeline, approvals)
- **Telemetry/SRE** to evaluate stop conditions and trigger auto-pause/rollback
- **Security/audit** to generate auditable change history of rollout actions

The orchestrator is designed to be deterministic, auditable, and resilient to partial failures (e.g., transient metric fetch failures, region propagation delays).

### Primary responsibilities
- Create and persist rollout objects (route + baseline + ...

title: Retention Controls Verification Dashboards & Alerts
preview:
## Purpose
This page defines the standard dashboards and alerting rules used to verify that **retention controls are enforced** across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). The primary verification signals are:
- **TTL lag**: how far behind the system is in deleting data that has expired.
- **Purg...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines the standard dashboards and alerting rules used to verify that **retention controls are enforced** across Redwood Inference products (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). The primary verification signals are:
- **TTL lag**: how far behind the system is in deleting data that has expired.
- **Purge job health**: errors, retries, and idempotency-safe progress.
- **Backlog size**: queued work and time-to-drain estimates.
- **Customer policy drift**: differences between configured retention policy and effective enforcement in the data plane.

These dashboards are required for GA of retention enforcement on any new data class/store (request/response logs, audit logs, traces/metrics, artifacts where applicable).

## Scope
Covers verification for retention enforcement across:
- **Request/response logs store** (tenant request logs)
- **Audit logs store** (security/audit ev...

title: Service Contracts Drift Detection and Healing: Operator Cookbook
preview:
Overview
This cookbook documents an operator-focused approach to detect, triage, and heal 'service contract' drift across the Redwood platform. 'Service contract' refers to the behavioral and performance guarantees a runtime component exposes to its callers: schema/response shape, established request/response semant...
text:
Overview
This cookbook documents an operator-focused approach to detect, triage, and heal 'service contract' drift across the Redwood platform. 'Service contract' refers to the behavioral and performance guarantees a runtime component exposes to its callers: schema/response shape, established request/response semantics (e.g., streaming vs single-shot), required headers/auth, and quantitative SLOs (latency, error-rate, throughput).

Goals
- Detect contract drift early (schema/regression/perf) before customer-facing features break.
- Automate low-risk healing steps to reduce toil and mean time to repair (MTTR).
- Provide clear operator playbooks for safe manual intervention when automation cannot resolve the drift.

Scope and non-goals
- This cookbook focuses on runtime and API contract drift between internal platform services and model-serving subsystems (allocator, runtime, cache).
- ...

## Purpose
This runbook covers day-2 operations for Redwood’s retention purge jobs (TTL enforcement) across supported data stores. It explains how to:
- Monitor purge health and retention “TTL lag”
- Safely re-run purge jobs (idempotent execution)
- Handle purge backlog without causing database pressure or telemetry regressions
- Verify deletions and produce internal evidence for audits/customer requests

This is the on-call reference for incidents like “TTL enforcement delayed in a region” or “purge backlog after maintenance”.

## Scope (what this runbook covers)
In-scope purge jobs (v1):
- Request/response logs store (a.k.a. request logs)
- Audit logs store
- Console traces (where enabled) and related metadata indexes
- Metrics retention (downsampling/expiration where applicable)

Out of scope (handled by separate runbooks or vendor lifecycle policies):
- Customer-managed object sto...

title: Retention Policy Service (RPS) — System Overview
preview:
## Overview
Retention Policy Service ("RPS") is the control-plane service responsible for storing, validating, and serving **customer-configurable retention policies** across Redwood Inference products (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private). RPS is the source of truth for retention configuration used by:
- TTL/purge worke...
text:
## Overview
Retention Policy Service ("RPS") is the control-plane service responsible for storing, validating, and serving **customer-configurable retention policies** across Redwood Inference products (Hosted API, Dedicated, Private). RPS is the source of truth for retention configuration used by:
- TTL/purge workers for request/response logs, audit logs, and other eligible data stores.
- Console retention settings UI (read/write + change history).
- Compliance Reporting Pack (retention configuration export attachments).

RPS does **not** delete data directly. It provides policy CRUD, evaluation, and auditable change history. Deletion enforcement is implemented in data-store-specific purge jobs and telemetry retention pipelines.

## Responsibilities (in scope)
1) Policy CRUD (create/update/read) per tenant and (where supported) per workspace/org.
2) Policy validation (tier constraint...

title: Rollback Procedure: Quantization Profiles (Route-level disable + Global Kill Switch)
preview:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to safely roll back Quantization Profile Library enablement when we observe quality regressions, latency/cost anomalies, error spikes, or compatibility issues. It covers:

* Disabling quantization at *route level* (preferred, least blast radius)
* Applying *tenant-level overrid...
text:
h1. Purpose
This runbook describes how to safely roll back Quantization Profile Library enablement when we observe quality regressions, latency/cost anomalies, error spikes, or compatibility issues. It covers:

* Disabling quantization at *route level* (preferred, least blast radius)
* Applying *tenant-level override* (if available/required)
* Triggering the *global kill switch* (emergency)
* Identifying impacted tenants/routes and confirming recovery via dashboards, logs, and traces

This runbook is intended for Eng Oncall, SRE, and Platform Control Plane oncall.

h1. Definitions (quick)
* *Quantization profile*: A versioned profile artifact applied by the serving runtime for an eligible model+hardware regime.
* *Route-level enablement*: Route config includes a quantization_profile_ref (or “default”) that can be toggled.
* *Fallback*: Policy engine/runtime chooses baseline precision ...

## Overview
This page specifies the monitoring, dashboards, and alert thresholds for **Hosted data residency enforcement** (region pinning + deterministic request-time enforcement).

The intent is to provide:
- Early detection of **policy enforcement regressions** (e.g., cross-region fallback attempts).
- Fast diagnosis for **customer-impacting false positives** (legitimate traffic blocked).
- Evidence-quality telemetry for Security & Compliance (auditability of enforcement decisions, without logging customer content).

This spec applies to:
- API Gateway (request validation + standardized residency error contract)
- Smart Routing (region selection + fallback constraints)
- Residency Policy Service (policy lookup/evaluation)
- Shared observability surfaces (dashboards, alerts, runbooks)

## Goals
1. Measure and alert on **blocked requests** caused by residency enforcement.
2. Measure ...

title: Residency Policy Service (RPS) — Control Plane System Documentation
preview:
## Overview
Residency Policy Service (RPS) is the control-plane system of record for **Hosted API data residency policies** (region pinning). RPS stores policy objects, enforces validation rules, provides deterministic policy evaluation for in-path services, and emits **audit events** for create/update/delete and ov...
text:
## Overview
Residency Policy Service (RPS) is the control-plane system of record for **Hosted API data residency policies** (region pinning). RPS stores policy objects, enforces validation rules, provides deterministic policy evaluation for in-path services, and emits **audit events** for create/update/delete and overrides.

RPS is part of the Enterprise Security & Compliance Foundation initiative: **Hosted data residency enforcement**. It enables:
- Customer-configurable **allowed region set** and **primary region** at org/project scope (and optionally API key).
- Deterministic request-time enforcement by downstream services (API Gateway + Smart Routing).
- Auditable configuration changes and operational observability.

RPS does **not** route traffic. It only provides configuration and a consistent evaluation contract.

## Service ownership
- Primary owner: Platform Control Plane (Et...

title: TP Stall and Timeout Dashboard (Tensor Parallel Runtime)
preview:

This page documents the *TP Stall and Timeout* dashboard used to detect and triage tensor-parallel (TP) stalls in the Serving Runtime. The dashboard is designed to answer three questions quickly:

1) *Are we stalling?* (heartbeat gaps / collective no-progress)

text:
h1. Overview
This page documents the *TP Stall and Timeout* dashboard used to detect and triage tensor-parallel (TP) stalls in the Serving Runtime. The dashboard is designed to answer three questions quickly:

1) *Are we stalling?* (heartbeat gaps / collective no-progress)
2) *Where is it happening?* (which replica, rank, model, cluster)
3) *What is the likely cause?* (compute vs comms/NCCL vs scheduler starvation)

The dashboard is used by *SRE on-call* and *Serving Runtime on-call* during incidents and canary rollouts (Dedicated-first, then Hosted).

h2. Ownership and support model
* Dashboard owner (eng-platform/observability): *Aisha Bello*
* Implementation (dashboards + alert rules): *Sergio Costa*
* Runtime instrumentation owners: *Omar El-Sayed* (metrics/logs), *Julian Moore* (scheduler + load-shed integration)
* On-call readiness / paging policy: *Rafael Mendes*

h3. Where the...

title: Dependency Fencing and Approximation Guide
preview:
Summary
-------
This document describes an operational pattern we call dependency fencing with approximation: proactive isolation of unhealthy or high-latency downstream services combined with controlled use of approximate responses to preserve SLOs and user experience. It provides design patterns, policy examples, ...
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes an operational pattern we call dependency fencing with approximation: proactive isolation of unhealthy or high-latency downstream services combined with controlled use of approximate responses to preserve SLOs and user experience. It provides design patterns, policy examples, runbook procedures, observability/playbook templates, and ownership guidance for platform teams that operate LLM inference and platform services.

Motivation
----------
- Many Redwood platform services depend on third-party or internal systems (e.g., feature stores, rerankers, external knowledge indexes). A single slow dependency can cascade into higher tail latency and SLO breaches.
- Full failover to a simpler model (e.g., locally cached embeddings) may improve latency but change output quality. We need predictable safety mechanisms that:
 - Minimize user-visible errors
 ...

title: How-to: Configure region pinning in staging (Hosted data residency enforcement)
preview:
## Overview
This page documents the staging-only procedure to:
- Enable Hosted data residency enforcement feature flags.

- Generate test API keys (including optional API-key-level overrides).

text:
## Overview
This page documents the staging-only procedure to:
- Enable Hosted data residency enforcement feature flags.
- Create or update a test org/project with a residency policy (allowed regions + primary region).
- Generate test API keys (including optional API-key-level overrides).
- Run verification scenarios to confirm:
 - Requests route only within the pinned region set.
 - Cross-region fallback is blocked by default.
 - Error responses are deterministic and match the public error contract.
 - Decision context is present in logs/metrics/audit events.

This is intended for engineers, SREs, and on-call validating rollouts before allowlisting customers.

---

## Terminology (staging)
- **Allowed regions**: The set of regions the request may be processed in (e.g., `eu-west-1`, `eu-central-1`).
- **Primary region**: Preferred region within the allowed set.
- **Cross-region fallba...

title: Hosted API model rollout runbook (fallback-enabled)
preview:
# Purpose
This runbook describes the **end-to-end procedure** for rolling out a new **Hosted API** model version when **automatic fallback** is enabled. The focus is correctness and fast recovery: we prefer a controlled canary and well-defined rollback/fallback behavior over speed.

# Prerequisites
- Release ticket ...
text:
# Purpose
This runbook describes the **end-to-end procedure** for rolling out a new **Hosted API** model version when **automatic fallback** is enabled. The focus is correctness and fast recovery: we prefer a controlled canary and well-defined rollback/fallback behavior over speed.

# Prerequisites
- Release ticket created (see Change Management page)
- Eval readiness gates met (see Applied ML gates page)
- Model artifacts present in registry (tagged RC)
- Fallback target model version identified and confirmed compatible

# Definitions
- **Primary model**: the new version being rolled out
- **Fallback model**: the version that traffic is routed to when fallback triggers
- **Route**: a Hosted API routing unit (by model name, region, tier, or customer pin)

# Step-by-step procedure
## 1) Validate configs in staging
1. Deploy the primary model to staging with the intended serving profile...

text:
## Purpose
This page is the canonical metric dictionary for the Internal Unit Economics Dashboard.

It defines:
- The primary KPI: **Blended $/1M output tokens**
- Inclusion/exclusion rules for both cost and tokens
- Dimension definitions used for drilldowns (model/version/quantization profile, region, customer tier)
- Interpretation guidance and common pitfalls

This document is used by Engineering, Product, SRE/Observability, and Finance to ensure the dashboard is consistent with FP&A reporting and reliable for exec-level usage.

---

## Primary KPI
### Metric name
**blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens_usd** (display name: **Blended $/1M output tokens**)

### Definition (high-level)
The blended cost metric answers:
> “For a given time window and slice (e.g., model/region/tier), what did it cost Redwood (USD) to produce 1,000,000 output tokens?”

This is a **fully-loaded, allocated** c...

preview:
## Purpose
This page is the on-call runbook for investigating discrepancies in the **Internal Unit Economics Dashboard** (primary metric: **blended $/1M output tokens**). It covers common failure modes, validation queries, backfill steps, and escalation guidance (including when Finance must be involved).

text:
## Purpose
This page is the on-call runbook for investigating discrepancies in the **Internal Unit Economics Dashboard** (primary metric: **blended $/1M output tokens**). It covers common failure modes, validation queries, backfill steps, and escalation guidance (including when Finance must be involved).

**Definition reminder:** The dashboard is intended to be finance-aligned and reconciliable to infra spend inputs + platform metering. If you are unsure whether you’re dealing with a *data pipeline issue* vs a *definition/methodology question*, follow the triage flow below.

## Scope
This runbook applies to:
- Missing/late dashboard data (freshness / completeness)
- Sudden drops/spikes in tokens or $/token
- Drilldown inconsistencies (by model/version/region/tier/quantization profile)
- Reconciliation gaps vs billing/FP&A totals (within agreed tolerance)

Non-scope:
- Customer-facing ...

title: Unit Economics Aggregation Service (UEAS) — System Overview
preview:
## Overview
The **Unit Economics Aggregation Service (UEAS)** is the hourly data pipeline that computes Redwood’s finance-aligned **blended cost per 1M output tokens** and related unit economics metrics. UEAS consumes token metering and cost attribution inputs (GPU rate card + allocation rules) and produces curated ...
text:
## Overview
The **Unit Economics Aggregation Service (UEAS)** is the hourly data pipeline that computes Redwood’s finance-aligned **blended cost per 1M output tokens** and related unit economics metrics. UEAS consumes token metering and cost attribution inputs (GPU rate card + allocation rules) and produces curated warehouse tables and views used by:

- **Console internal Unit Economics dashboard** (filters + drilldowns)
- **Finance reconciliation and KPI reporting**
- **Engineering diagnostics** (cache/batching/quantization impact)

UEAS is designed to be:
- **Idempotent** per hour partition
- **Auditable** (rate card versioning + allocation version)
- **Stable in cardinality** (dimension guardrails)
- **Operationalized** (SLOs + alerting + runbooks)

---

## Ownership & On-call
**Primary owner team:** Telemetry & Analytics (Eng Platform)

**DRI (engineering):** Logan Wright (EM, Tel...

title: Service Decommission & Ownership Tracker (Platform How‑To)
preview:
Summary:

This document describes the platform-level process for assigning and tracking service ownership, performing safe decommissions, and tracking handoffs during Kubernetes migrations. It is intended for platform engineers, service owners, and SREs. The goal is to reduce orphaned services, ensure clear runbook ...
text:
Summary:

This document describes the platform-level process for assigning and tracking service ownership, performing safe decommissions, and tracking handoffs during Kubernetes migrations. It is intended for platform engineers, service owners, and SREs. The goal is to reduce orphaned services, ensure clear runbook coverage, and provide a reproducible decommission flow that leaves audit artifacts and rollback options.

Scope:
- Applies to all services running on Redwood-managed clusters (production, staging, dev).
- Includes microservices, batch jobs, cronjobs, and infrastructure-only deployments (e.g., shared exporters).
- Excludes vendor-managed SaaS where Redwood does not control runtime (see exception guidance below).

Why this matters:
- Orphaned services increase security and cost risk.
- Unclear ownership delays incident response and blocks rollbacks.
- Safe decommissioning red...

text:
# Overview
This page explains how to manually shift production inference traffic between regions/clusters by applying routing overrides in the Router/Policy Service.

# When to use
- Regional degradation or cloud incident
- GPU capacity shortage in a region/cluster
- Elevated 5xx or sustained p95 latency regression

# What this changes
- Route weights for new requests only
- Optional: disabling a route group entirely (hard failover)
- Optional: forcing routing to a specific cluster for containment

# Guardrails
- Only the incident commander or oncall lead should approve production overrides
- Overrides must be time-bounded (set an expiry)
- Always leave a rollback note in the incident channel

# Inputs
- policy_name: hosted-api-prod (primary)
- route_group: one of chat, embeddings, rerank
- selector: tier=standard|enterprise; region preference if relevant

# Procedure
1) Confirm capac...

title: Hosted model rollout and fallback runbook
preview:
# Overview
This runbook describes the standard procedure to roll out a new model to the Hosted API catalog and configure safe fallback/rollback. It is written for Release Engineering, Platform, and on-call responders.

# Preconditions (must be true before rollout)
- Model intake ticket exists and is in `ready-for-ro...
text:
# Overview
This runbook describes the standard procedure to roll out a new model to the Hosted API catalog and configure safe fallback/rollback. It is written for Release Engineering, Platform, and on-call responders.

# Preconditions (must be true before rollout)
- Model intake ticket exists and is in `ready-for-rollout` status.
- Applied ML has provided eval gates pass results and a one-paragraph risk assessment.
- Serving Runtime has signed off the performance bar results for the default profile.
- Security has approved the third-party model risk review (or an exception is linked).

# Roles
- Rollout owner: Release Engineering
- Routing change owner: Platform
- On-call: Eng Oncall (runtime + platform)

# Required artifacts
- GitHub PR in `redwood-model-registry` adding the model and quant profiles
- Perf suite run IDs (default profile)
- Eval harness run links (Suites A + any tool-...

text:
# Purpose
This page lists known **credential dependencies** for Redwood control plane and core production services. Use it to ensure secret rotation is complete (i.e., every consumer is updated).

# How to use this inventory
- For a given secret, identify all services that read it.
- Confirm whether the service reloads secrets dynamically or requires a restart.
- Confirm whether the secret supports a dual-valid overlap window.

# Inventory table
Format: Service | Secret name/path | Used for | Reload behavior | Notes

- api-gateway | vault:prod/api-gateway/jwt-signing-key | signing outbound session tokens | requires deploy | overlap supported via key ring
- router | vault:prod/router/service-token | service-to-service auth | hot reload every 5 min | retry storms possible if token invalid
- billing-meter | vault:prod/billing/warehouse-creds | analytics warehouse access | requires restar...

title: SLI contract friction and cross-team learning sprint retrospective (Nov 2024)
preview:
Overview:

This retrospective captures the outcomes from the two-week cross-team learning sprint (Nov 4–18, 2024) that emerged after multiple small but persistent SLI contract breaks across platform components. The goal of the sprint was to: (1) surface where SLI definitions and alert contracts diverged between team...
text:
Overview:

This retrospective captures the outcomes from the two-week cross-team learning sprint (Nov 4–18, 2024) that emerged after multiple small but persistent SLI contract breaks across platform components. The goal of the sprint was to: (1) surface where SLI definitions and alert contracts diverged between teams, (2) produce concrete dashboard and alert changes that reduce noisy page wakes, and (3) update operator-facing runbooks to speed diagnosis.

Summary of findings:
- Multiple teams (allocator, prefetch-controller, router, kv-prefix-cache) had subtly different interpretations of the same SLIs (e.g., \"token_cost_per_request\" vs \"token_cost_per_user_request\").
- Alerts were firing during legitimate transient load patterns due to brittle burn-rate thresholds and missing context panels (tenant, region, model_variant).
- Operators lacked a compact decision canvas that maps an...
